evidence: BlackLabQueryExecutor wraps InvalidQuery and all low-level exceptions into IOException with original cause preserved throughout (lines 138, 177, 208, 242, 425, 502) — consistent chaining pattern in the query layer.
WordSketchApiServer routes catch IOException and translate to HTTP 500 via sendError() — consistent at the API boundary for most handlers.

getTotalFrequency in BlackLabQueryExecutor: 'catch (Exception e) { logger.warn(...); return 0; }' — broad catch swallows the exception and silently returns 0, making callers unable to distinguish index failure from absent lemma; logDice scores become silently incorrect.
Main.java shutdown hook: 'catch (IOException e) { // Ignore }' — silent swallow of close() failures at cleanup boundary.

WordSketchApiServer inconsistently catches exceptions across sibling route wrappers: /api/semantic-field/explore and /api/semantic-field/explore-multi wrap both IOException and Exception, while /api/semantic-field, /api/semantic-field/examples, and /api/concordance/examples wrap only IOException — an unchecked RuntimeException from those handlers would propagate to the raw HttpServer and crash the request silently.

handleBcqlQueryPost catches generic Exception and sends HTTP 400 ('BCQL query error: ' + e.getMessage()) regardless of whether the failure is a parse error or an I/O failure — conflates client error (400) with server error (500).
ExplorationHandlers consistently catches NumberFormatException and returns 400 with message — correct client error handling at validation boundary.

strengths: The query layer (BlackLabQueryExecutor) applies a single consistent strategy: wrap any library exception into IOException with original cause, propagate upward — no silent swallows at the execution level.
All API handlers validate required parameters and return 400 with descriptive messages before touching the executor.
HTTP-level error responses always use the same sendError(exchange, code, message) utility — format is consistent.
error_consistency.issue_character: The codebase has a well-designed error-propagation spine in the query layer, but the API routing layer has two failure modes: some routes swallow RuntimeExceptions silently, and one utility method (getTotalFrequency) returns a default value on failure instead of propagating, corrupting downstream logDice scores.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The query layer scores high on consistency, applying IOException wrapping uniformly. However, getTotalFrequency's silent-zero return is a meaningful correctness issue (not just a consistency smell), and the inconsistent RuntimeException coverage across sibling route wrappers creates unpredictable failure modes — some endpoints will surface errors as 500, others will silently fail. These two issues drag the score below 60. A fully consistent codebase would propagate or explicitly document every exception boundary.

issues.summary: getTotalFrequency swallows exceptions and returns 0, silently corrupting logDice scores
evidence: catch (Exception e) { logger.warn(...); return 0; } in getTotalFrequency — index failures are indistinguishable from zero-frequency lemmas, making logDice silently incorrect for all callers
issues.suggestion: Propagate the exception as IOException so callers can decide whether to skip or surface the error; alternatively, use a sentinel value (e.g. -1) with documented semantics and check it at all call sites.

issues.summary: Sibling API route wrappers inconsistently catch RuntimeException — some routes will silently crash on unexpected errors
evidence: /api/semantic-field/explore catches IOException + Exception; /api/semantic-field catches only IOException — an unexpected NullPointerException from handleSemanticField would propagate uncaught
issues.suggestion: Standardise all route wrappers to catch both IOException and a top-level Exception with a 500 response, or extract a shared wrapHandler() utility that handles both cases uniformly.

issues.summary: handleBcqlQueryPost sends HTTP 400 for all exceptions including I/O failures that should be 500
evidence: catch (Exception e) { logger.error(...); HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 400, 'BCQL query error: ' + e.getMessage()); } — IOException from index access maps to 400 Bad Request, misleading clients
issues.suggestion: Differentiate: catch InvalidQuery / IllegalArgumentException and send 400; catch IOException separately and send 500.

issues.summary: Main shutdown hook silently ignores IOException from executor.close()
evidence: Runtime.getRuntime().addShutdownHook(new Thread(() -> { ... try { executor.close(); } catch (IOException e) { // Ignore } }));
issues.suggestion: At minimum log the exception at warn/error level so operator knows close failed; for a JVM shutdown the swallow may be acceptable but should be documented.

root_causes: The query layer was designed with a single clear error strategy (IOException wrapping), but the API routing layer was built incrementally, leading to inconsistent catch coverage across sibling handlers.
getTotalFrequency returns a default to avoid breaking callers in a hot path, but this trades correctness for resilience without documenting the tradeoff.
